revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''津島の田植踊'''（つしまのたうえおどり）は、[[福島県]][[双葉郡]][[浪江町]]津島に伝わる民俗芸能の[[田植踊]]である...'
revision.text:
'''津島の田植踊'''（つしまのたうえおどり）は、[[福島県]][[双葉郡]][[浪江町]]津島に伝わる民俗芸能の[[田植踊]]である。国の[[選択無形民俗文化財]]・福島県指定[[無形民俗文化財]]。
この田植踊は浪江町津島の、上津島、下津島、南津島、赤宇木の、4つの[[集落]]に伝わる[[豊作]]祈願の[[予祝芸能]]であり、かっては[[旧暦]]の[[1月14日 (旧暦)|1月14日]]・[[1月15日 (旧暦)|15日]]・[[1月17日 (旧暦)|17日]]に行われていたが、現在は新暦で行われ、初日の14日に各集落のそれぞれの[[神社]]に[[奉納]]された後、依頼された家々を巡って踊られる。
[[長襦袢]]を着て[[緞子]]を締め[[手拭]]のねじり[[鉢巻]]をして大きなふくべを持った「鍬頭」、[[紋付羽織袴]]で[[脇差]]をさし[[扇子]]を持って[[歌]]をうたう「種下ろし」2人、[[紋付]]の[[袷]]に奴帯を締めて尻をはしょり白[[股引]]に[[脚絆]]を付け[[太鼓]]と[[撥|バチ]]を持った「太鼓打ち」2人、[[絣]]の着物で尻をはしょり[[ささら]]を持った「ささら摺り」2人と、数人の江戸褄を着ておこそ頭巾をかぶり、四つ竹・扇子を持った「早乙女」によって演じられるが、このうち「ささら摺り」のみ14～5歳の[[少年]]で、あとは全員成年男性である。
鍬頭の口上に始まり、「種まき」「田植え」「稲刈り」「稲扱き」「籾摺り」「唐箕吹き」「上がりはか」などの曲目が踊られ、最後に鍬頭が祝言を述べる。
== 参考文献 == * 『祭・芸能・行事大辞典（下）』（朝倉書店、2009年）ISBN 978-4-254-50013-4
{{DEFAULTSORT:つしまのたたうえおとり}} [[Category:福島県の祭り]] [[category:浪江町]] [[Category:都道府県指定無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:日本国選択無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:予祝芸能]] {{japan-culture-stub}}

revision.comment: Created page with "'''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a traditional folk performance of rice planting dance from {{ill|Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja|福島県双葉郡浪江町津島}}, Japan. It is recognized as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a prefectural {{ill|Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} of Fukushima Prefecture. This rice planting dance i..."
revision.text:
'''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a traditional folk performance of rice planting dance from {{ill|Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja|福島県双葉郡浪江町津島}}, Japan. It is recognized as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a prefectural {{ill|Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} of Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice planting dance is a form of {{ill|Yoshuku performing arts|ja|予祝芸能}} that prays for abundant harvests, passed down through four villages in Namie: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akugun. It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}, followed by performances at houses upon request.
The performance features distinct roles: - **Kuwagashira** (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a {{ill|Tenugui|ja|手拭}} around their head, and carries a large calabash. - **Taneoroshi** (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴}}, equipped with short swords and folding fans, who sing. - **Taiko-uchi** (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. - **Sasara-suri** (ささら摺り): Two performers in {{ill|Kasuri|ja|絣}} kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo clappers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. - **Saotome** (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion." It concludes with the Kuwagashira delivering a celebratory message.
== References == * *Matsuri, Geinō, Gyōji Daijiten (Part 2)*, Asakura Shoten, 2009, ISBN 978-4-254-50013-4
== External links == * {{Cultural Asset Database|312|243|Tsushima no Taue Odori}}

It is recognized as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a prefectural {{ill|Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} of Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice planting dance is a form of {{ill|Yoshuku performing arts|ja|予祝芸能}} that prays for abundant harvests, passed down through four villages in Namie: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akugun. It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}, followed by performances at houses upon request.

These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. - **Saotome** (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.

'''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a traditional folk performance of rice planting dance from {{ill|Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja|福島県双葉郡浪江町津島}}, Japan. It is recognized as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a prefectural {{ill|Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} of Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice planting dance is a form of {{ill|Yoshuku performing arts|ja|予祝芸能}} that prays for abundant harvests, passed down through four villages in Namie: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akugun. It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}, followed by performances at houses upon request.
The performance features distinct roles: - **Kuwagashira** (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a {{ill|Tenugui|ja|手拭}} around their head, and carries a large calabash. - **Taneoroshi** (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴}}, equipped with short swords and folding fans, who sing. - **Taiko-uchi** (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. - **Sasara-suri** (ささら摺り): Two performers in {{ill|Kasuri|ja|絣}} kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo clappers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. - **Saotome** (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion." It concludes with the Kuwagashira delivering a celebratory message.

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:original pages]][[category:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[category:original pages]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a traditional folk performance of rice planting dance from {{ill|Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja|福島県双葉郡浪江町津島}}, Japan. It is recognized as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a prefectural {{ill|Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} of Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice planting dance is a form of {{ill|Yoshuku performing arts|ja|予祝芸能}} that prays for abundant harvests, passed down through four villages in Namie: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akugun. It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}, followed by performances at houses upon request.
The performance features distinct roles: - '''Kuwagashira''' (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a {{ill|Tenugui|ja|手拭}} around their head, and carries a large calabash. - '''Taneoroshi''' (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴}}, equipped with short swords and folding fans, who sing. - '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. - '''Sasara-suri''' (ささら摺り): Two performers in {{ill|Kasuri|ja|絣}} kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo clappers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. - '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion." It concludes with the Kuwagashira delivering a celebratory message.
== References == * *Matsuri, Geinō, Gyōji Daijiten (Part 2)*, Asakura Shoten, 2009, ISBN 978-4-254-50013-4
== External links == * {{Cultural Asset Database|312|243|Tsushima no Taue Odori}}

'''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a traditional folk performance of rice planting dance from {{ill|Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja|福島県双葉郡浪江町津島}}, Japan. It is recognized as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a prefectural {{ill|Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} of Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice planting dance is a form of {{ill|Yoshuku performing arts|ja|予祝芸能}} that prays for abundant harvests, passed down through four villages in Namie: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akugun. It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}, followed by performances at houses upon request.
The performance features distinct roles: - '''Kuwagashira''' (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a {{ill|Tenugui|ja|手拭}} around their head, and carries a large calabash. - '''Taneoroshi''' (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴}}, equipped with short swords and folding fans, who sing. - '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. - '''Sasara-suri''' (ささら摺り): Two performers in {{ill|Kasuri|ja|絣}} kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo clappers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. - '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion." It concludes with the Kuwagashira delivering a celebratory message.
== References == * *Matsuri, Geinō, Gyōji Daijiten (Part 2)*, Asakura Shoten, 2009, ISBN 978-4-254-50013-4
== External links == * {{Cultural Asset Database|312|243|Tsushima no Taue Odori}}

'''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a traditional folk performance of rice planting dance from {{ill|Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja|福島県双葉郡浪江町津島}}, Japan. It is recognized as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a prefectural {{ill|Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} of Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice planting dance is a form of {{ill|Yoshuku performing arts|ja|予祝芸能}} that prays for abundant harvests, passed down through four villages in Namie: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akugun. It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}, followed by performances at houses upon request.
The performance features distinct roles: - '''Kuwagashira''' (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a {{ill|Tenugui|ja|手拭}} around their head, and carries a large calabash. - '''Taneoroshi''' (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴}}, equipped with short swords and folding fans, who sing. - '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. - '''Sasara-suri''' (ささら摺り): Two performers in {{ill|Kasuri|ja|絣}} kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo clappers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. - '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion." It concludes with the Kuwagashira delivering a celebratory message.
== References == * *Matsuri, Geinō, Gyōji Daijiten (Part 2)*, Asakura Shoten, 2009, ISBN 978-4-254-50013-4
== External links == * {{Cultural Asset Database|312|243|Tsushima no Taue Odori}}

The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion." It concludes with the Kuwagashira delivering a celebratory message.

This rice planting dance is a form of {{ill|Yoshuku performing arts|ja|予祝芸能}} that prays for abundant harvests, passed down through four villages in Namie: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akugun. It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:qq-" to "[[Category:deprecated qq open]]<!--"
revision.text:
'''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a traditional folk performance of rice planting dance from {{ill|Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja|福島県双葉郡浪江町津島}}, Japan. It is recognized as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a prefectural {{ill|Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} of Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice planting dance is a form of {{ill|Yoshuku performing arts|ja|予祝芸能}} that prays for abundant harvests, passed down through four villages in Namie: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akugun. It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}, followed by performances at houses upon request.
The performance features distinct roles: - '''Kuwagashira''' (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a {{ill|Tenugui|ja|手拭}} around their head, and carries a large calabash. - '''Taneoroshi''' (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴}}, equipped with short swords and folding fans, who sing. - '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. - '''Sasara-suri''' (ささら摺り): Two performers in {{ill|Kasuri|ja|絣}} kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo clappers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. - '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion." It concludes with the Kuwagashira delivering a celebratory message.
== References == * *Matsuri, Geinō, Gyōji Daijiten (Part 2)*, Asakura Shoten, 2009, ISBN 978-4-254-50013-4
== External links == * {{Cultural Asset Database|312|243|Tsushima no Taue Odori}}

The performance features distinct roles: - '''Kuwagashira''' (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a {{ill|Tenugui|ja|手拭}} around their head, and carries a large calabash. - '''Taneoroshi''' (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴}}, equipped with short swords and folding fans, who sing. - '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. - '''Sasara-suri''' (ささら摺り): Two performers in {{ill|Kasuri|ja|絣}} kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo clappers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. - '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion." It concludes with the Kuwagashira delivering a celebratory message.

On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}, followed by performances at houses upon request.
The performance features distinct roles: * '''Kuwagashira''' (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a {{ill|Tenugui|ja|手拭}} around their head, and carries a large calabash. * '''Taneoroshi''' (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴}}, equi11ed with short swords and folding fans, who sing. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. * '''Sasara-suri''' (ささら摺り): Two performers in {{ill|Kasuri|ja|絣}} kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo cla11ers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion." It concludes with the Kuwagashira delivering a celebratory message.

It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}, followed by performances at houses upon request.
The performance features distinct roles: * '''Kuwagashira''' (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a {{ill|Tenugui|ja|手拭}} around their head, and carries a large calabash. * '''Taneoroshi''' (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴}}, equi11ed with short swords and folding fans, who sing. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. * '''Sasara-suri''' (ささら摺り): Two performers in {{ill|Kasuri|ja|絣}} kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo cla11ers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion tier 3]][[Category:." It concludes with the Kuwagashira delivering a celebratory message.

The performance features distinct roles: * '''Kuwagashira''' (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢|12=draft}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a {{ill|Tenugui|ja|手拭|12=draft}} around their head, and carries a large calabash. * '''Taneoroshi''' (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴|12=draft}}, equi11ed with short swords and folding fans, who sing. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯|12=draft}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引|12=draft}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. * '''Sasara-suri''' (ささら摺り): Two performers in {{ill|Kasuri|ja|絣|12=draft}} kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo cla11ers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾|12=draft}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion tier 3]]

revision.text:
'''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a traditional folk performance of rice planting dance from {{ill|Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja|福島県双葉郡浪江町津島|12=draft}}, Japan. It is recognized as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=draft}} by the national government and as a prefectural {{ill|Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=draft}} of Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice planting dance is a form of {{ill|Yoshuku performing arts|ja|予祝芸能|12=draft}} that prays for abundant harvests, passed down through four villages in Namie: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akugun. It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)|12=draft}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|12=draft}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)|12=draft}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}}, followed by performances at houses upon request.
The performance features distinct roles: * '''Kuwagashira''' (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢|12=draft}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a {{ill|Tenugui|ja|手拭|12=draft}} around their head, and carries a large calabash. * '''Taneoroshi''' (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴|12=draft}}, equi11ed with short swords and folding fans, who sing. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯|12=draft}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引|12=draft}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. * '''Sasara-suri''' (ささら摺り): Two performers in {{ill|Kasuri|ja|絣|12=draft}} kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo cla11ers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾|12=draft}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion tier 3]]

revision.text:
'''津島の田植踊'''（つしまのたうえおどり）は、[[福島県]][[双葉郡]][[浪江町]]津島に伝わる民俗芸能の[[田植踊]]である。国の[[選択無形民俗文化財]]・福島県指定[[無形民俗文化財]]。
この田植踊は浪江町津島の、上津島、下津島、南津島、赤宇木の、4つの[[集落]]に伝わる[[豊作]]祈願の[[予祝芸能]]であり、かっては[[旧暦]]の[[1月14日 (旧暦)|1月14日]]・[[1月15日 (旧暦)|15日]]・[[1月17日 (旧暦)|17日]]に行われていたが、現在は新暦で行われ、初日の14日に各集落のそれぞれの[[神社]]に[[奉納]]された後、依頼された家々を巡って踊られる。
[[長襦袢]]を着て[[緞子]]を締め[[手拭]]のねじり[[鉢巻]]をして大きなふくべを持った「鍬頭」、[[紋付羽織袴]]で[[脇差]]をさし[[扇子]]を持って[[歌]]をうたう「種下ろし」2人、[[紋付]]の[[袷]]に奴帯を締めて尻をはしょり白[[股引]]に[[脚絆]]を付け[[太鼓]]と[[撥|バチ]]を持った「太鼓打ち」2人、[[絣]]の着物で尻をはしょり[[ささら]]を持った「ささら摺り」2人と、数人の江戸褄を着ておこそ頭巾をかぶり、四つ竹・扇子を持った「早乙女」によって演じられるが、このうち「ささら摺り」のみ14～5歳の[[少年]]で、あとは全員成年男性である。
鍬頭の口上に始まり、「種まき」「田植え」「稲刈り」「稲扱き」「籾摺り」「唐箕吹き」「上がりはか」などの曲目が踊られ、最後に鍬頭が祝言を述べる。
== 参考文献 == * 『祭・芸能・行事大辞典（下）』（朝倉書店、2009年）ISBN 978-4-254-50013-4
== 外部リンク == *{{国指定文化財等データベース|312|243|津島の田植踊}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:つしまのたたうえおとり}} [[Category:東北地方開催の冬季イベント]] [[Category:福島県の祭り]] [[category:浪江町]] [[Category:都道府県指定無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財]] [[Category:予祝芸能]] {{japan-culture-stub}}

revision.text:
'''津島の田植踊'''（つしまのたうえおどり）は、[[福島県]][[双葉郡]][[浪江町]]津島に伝わる民俗芸能の[[田植踊]]である。国の[[選択無形民俗文化財]]・福島県指定[[無形民俗文化財]]。
この田植踊は浪江町津島の、上津島、下津島、南津島、赤宇木の、4つの[[集落]]に伝わる[[豊作]]祈願の[[予祝芸能]]であり、かっては[[旧暦]]の[[1月14日 (旧暦)|1月14日]]・[[1月15日 (旧暦)|15日]]・[[1月17日 (旧暦)|17日]]に行われていたが、現在は新暦で行われ、初日の14日に各集落のそれぞれの[[神社]]に[[奉納]]された後、依頼された家々を巡って踊られる。
[[長襦袢]]を着て[[緞子]]を締め[[手拭]]のねじり[[鉢巻]]をして大きなふくべを持った「鍬頭」、[[紋付羽織袴]]で[[脇差]]をさし[[扇子]]を持って[[歌]]をうたう「種下ろし」2人、[[紋付]]の[[袷]]に奴帯を締めて尻をはしょり白[[股引]]に[[脚絆]]を付け[[太鼓]]と[[撥|バチ]]を持った「太鼓打ち」2人、[[絣]]の着物で尻をはしょり[[ささら]]を持った「ささら摺り」2人と、数人の江戸褄を着ておこそ頭巾をかぶり、四つ竹・扇子を持った「早乙女」によって演じられるが、このうち「ささら摺り」のみ14～5歳の[[少年]]で、あとは全員成年男性である。
鍬頭の口上に始まり、「種まき」「田植え」「稲刈り」「稲扱き」「籾摺り」「唐箕吹き」「上がりはか」などの曲目が踊られ、最後に鍬頭が祝言を述べる。
== 参考文献 == * 『祭・芸能・行事大辞典（下）』（朝倉書店、2009年）ISBN 978-4-254-50013-4

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:津島の田植踊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11556060]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a traditional folk performance of rice planting dance from {{ill|Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja|福島県双葉郡浪江町津島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Japan. It is recognized as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a prefectural {{ill|Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} of Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice planting dance is a form of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku performing arts|ja|予祝芸能|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshuku performing arts|qq=}} that prays for abundant harvests, passed down through four villages in Namie: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akugun. It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 14th (lunar calendar)|qq=|1=14th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839082}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 15th (lunar calendar)|qq=|1=15th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838395}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 17th (lunar calendar)|qq=|1=17th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838983}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shrine]], followed by performances at houses upon request.
The performance features distinct roles: * '''Kuwagashira''' (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagajuban|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 襦袢|1=Zhongyi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55358825}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a [[:en:Tenugui|Tenugui]] around their head, and carries a large calabash. * '''Taneoroshi''' (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mon-tsuki haori hakama|qq=|1=montsuki-haori-hakama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605315}}, equi11ed with short swords and folding fans, who sing. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaku-himo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matabiki|qq=|1=momohiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611516}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. * '''Sasara-suri''' (ささら摺り): Two performers in [[:en:Kasuri|Kasuri]] kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo cla11ers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okoso zukin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion tier 3]] {{Japan-culture-stub}}

[[ja:津島の田植踊]]
<!--Tsubame Taue Odori--><!--Tsubame Taue Odori--><!--Tsubame Taue Odori-->
[[Category:pages with merged translations]] ==Merged second translation== '''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice-planting dance|qq=}}, a form of folk performing art, passed down in {{ill|Tsunami, Namie|ja|浪江町津島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunami, Namie|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Namie, Fukushima|Namie, Fukushima]], located in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]], Japan. It has been designated as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a {{ill|Prefectural Cultural Property of Japan|ja|都道府県指定無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Cultural Property of Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice-planting dance is a {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝芸能|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|qq=}} (ritual performance for blessings of bountiful harvests) originating from four [[:en:Human settlement|settlements]] in Tsushima: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akaogi. It was traditionally performed on the {{ill|14th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/14th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}, {{ill|15th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/15th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}, and {{ill|17th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/17th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}. Today, the dance is performed according to the Gregorian calendar. On the 14th, it is first offered at each settlement's [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]], followed by performances at requested households.
The performers include: * '''Kuwakashira''' (鍬頭): Dressed in {{ill|naga-juban|ja|長襦袢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/naga-juban|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 襦袢|1=Zhongyi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55358825}} and tied with {{ill|donko|ja|緞子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/donko|qq=|1=donko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11607976}}, wearing a twisted [[:en:Tenugui|tenugui]] headband and carrying a large gourd. * '''Tane-oroshi''' (種下ろし): Two singers dressed in {{ill|formal kimono|ja|紋付羽織袴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/formal kimono|qq=|1=montsuki-haori-hakama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605315}}, armed with a [[:en:Wakizashi|wakizashi]] and holding [[:en:Hand fan|sensu]]. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two drummers wearing {{ill|formal hakama|ja|袷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/formal hakama|qq=|1=awase|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626642}} tied with a {{ill|yaku-obi|ja|奴帯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yaku-obi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with rolled-up kimono hems, white {{ill|mohimo|ja|股引|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mohimo|qq=|1=momohiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611516}}, and [[:en:Kyahan|Kyahan]], carrying [[:en:Drum|taiko]] and sticks. * '''Sasara-zuri''' (ささら摺り): Two boys aged 14–15 wearing [[:en:Kasuri|kasuri]] garments, equipped with [[:en:Binzasara|Binzasara]] (wooden clappers). * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several adult male performers in {{ill|Edo-tsuma|ja|江戸褄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo-tsuma|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} kimono, wearing {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okoso zukin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} hoods and holding {{ill|yotsutake|ja|四つ竹|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yotsutake|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or sensu.
The performance begins with a speech by the Kuwakashira, followed by dance sequences symbolizing "Seed Sowing," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Milling," "Winnowing," and "Ending Ceremony." The ritual concludes with the Kuwakashira delivering congratulatory remarks.
== References == * "Matsuri, Geinō, Gyōji Daijiten (Vol. 2)" (朝倉書店, 2009), ISBN 978-4-254-50013-4.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:津島の田植踊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11556060]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a traditional folk performance of rice planting dance from {{ill|Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja|福島県双葉郡浪江町津島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Japan. It is recognized as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a prefectural {{ill|Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} of Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice planting dance is a form of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku performing arts|ja|予祝芸能|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshuku performing arts|qq=}} that prays for abundant harvests, passed down through four villages in Namie: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akugun. It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 14th (lunar calendar)|1=14th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839082}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 15th (lunar calendar)|1=15th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838395}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 17th (lunar calendar)|1=17th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838983}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shrine]], followed by performances at houses upon request.
The performance features distinct roles: * '''Kuwagashira''' (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagajuban|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 襦袢|1=Zhongyi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55358825}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a [[:en:Tenugui|Tenugui]] around their head, and carries a large calabash. * '''Taneoroshi''' (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mon-tsuki haori hakama|1=montsuki-haori-hakama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605315}}, equi11ed with short swords and folding fans, who sing. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaku-himo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matabiki|1=momohiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611516}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. * '''Sasara-suri''' (ささら摺り): Two performers in [[:en:Kasuri|Kasuri]] kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo cla11ers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okoso zukin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion tier 3]] {{Japan-culture-stub}}
}}

[[Category:pages with merged translations]] ==Merged second translation== '''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice-planting dance|qq=}}, a form of folk performing art, passed down in {{ill|Tsunami, Namie|ja|浪江町津島|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunami, Namie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Namie, Fukushima|Namie, Fukushima]], located in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]], Japan. It has been designated as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a {{ill|Prefectural Cultural Property of Japan|ja|都道府県指定無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Cultural Property of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice-planting dance is a {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝芸能|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|qq=}} (ritual performance for blessings of bountiful harvests) originating from four [[:en:Human settlement|settlements]] in Tsushima: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akaogi. It was traditionally performed on the {{ill|14th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/14th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}, {{ill|15th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/15th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}, and {{ill|17th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/17th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}. Today, the dance is performed according to the Gregorian calendar. On the 14th, it is first offered at each settlement's [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]], followed by performances at requested households.
The performers include: * '''Kuwakashira''' (鍬頭): Dressed in {{ill|naga-juban|ja|長襦袢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/naga-juban|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 襦袢|1=Zhongyi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55358825}} and tied with {{ill|donko|ja|緞子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/donko|1=donko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11607976}}, wearing a twisted [[:en:Tenugui|tenugui]] headband and carrying a large gourd. * '''Tane-oroshi''' (種下ろし): Two singers dressed in {{ill|formal kimono|ja|紋付羽織袴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/formal kimono|1=montsuki-haori-hakama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605315}}, armed with a [[:en:Wakizashi|wakizashi]] and holding [[:en:Hand fan|sensu]]. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two drummers wearing {{ill|formal hakama|ja|袷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/formal hakama|1=awase|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626642}} tied with a {{ill|yaku-obi|ja|奴帯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yaku-obi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with rolled-up kimono hems, white {{ill|mohimo|ja|股引|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mohimo|1=momohiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611516}}, and [[:en:Kyahan|Kyahan]], carrying [[:en:Drum|taiko]] and sticks. * '''Sasara-zuri''' (ささら摺り): Two boys aged 14–15 wearing [[:en:Kasuri|kasuri]] garments, equipped with [[:en:Binzasara|Binzasara]] (wooden clappers). * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several adult male performers in {{ill|Edo-tsuma|ja|江戸褄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo-tsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} kimono, wearing {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okoso zukin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} hoods and holding {{ill|yotsutake|ja|四つ竹|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yotsutake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or sensu.
The performance begins with a speech by the Kuwakashira, followed by dance sequences symbolizing "Seed Sowing," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Milling," "Winnowing," and "Ending Ceremony." The ritual concludes with the Kuwakashira delivering congratulatory remarks.
== References == * "Matsuri, Geinō, Gyōji Daijiten (Vol. 2)" (朝倉書店, 2009), ISBN 978-4-254-50013-4.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:津島の田植踊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11556060]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a traditional folk performance of rice planting dance from {{ill|Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja|福島県双葉郡浪江町津島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Japan. It is recognized as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a prefectural {{ill|Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} of Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice planting dance is a form of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku performing arts|ja|予祝芸能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshuku performing arts|qq=}} that prays for abundant harvests, passed down through four villages in Namie: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akugun. It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 14th (lunar calendar)|1=14th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839082}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 15th (lunar calendar)|1=15th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838395}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 17th (lunar calendar)|1=17th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838983}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shrine]], followed by performances at houses upon request.
The performance features distinct roles: * '''Kuwagashira''' (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagajuban|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 襦袢|1=Zhongyi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55358825}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a [[:en:Tenugui|Tenugui]] around their head, and carries a large calabash. * '''Taneoroshi''' (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mon-tsuki haori hakama|1=montsuki-haori-hakama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605315}}, equi11ed with short swords and folding fans, who sing. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaku-himo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matabiki|1=momohiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611516}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. * '''Sasara-suri''' (ささら摺り): Two performers in [[:en:Kasuri|Kasuri]] kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo cla11ers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okoso zukin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion tier 3]] {{Japan-culture-stub}}
}}

It was traditionally performed on the {{ill|14th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/14th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}, {{ill|15th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/15th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}, and {{ill|17th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/17th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}. Today, the dance is performed according to the Gregorian calendar. On the 14th, it is first offered at each settlement's [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]], followed by performances at requested households.
The performers include: * '''Kuwakashira''' (鍬頭): Dressed in {{ill|naga-juban|ja|長襦袢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/naga-juban|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 襦袢|1=Zhongyi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55358825}} and tied with {{ill|donko|ja|緞子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/donko|1=donko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11607976}}, wearing a twisted [[:en:Tenugui|tenugui]] headband and carrying a large gourd. * '''Tane-oroshi''' (種下ろし): Two singers dressed in {{ill|formal kimono|ja|紋付羽織袴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/formal kimono|1=montsuki-haori-hakama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605315}}, armed with a [[:en:Wakizashi|wakizashi]] and holding [[:en:Hand fan|sensu]]. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two drummers wearing {{ill|formal hakama|ja|袷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/formal hakama|1=awase|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626642}} tied with a {{ill|yaku-obi|ja|奴帯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yaku-obi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with rolled-up kimono hems, white {{ill|mohimo|ja|股引|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mohimo|1=momohiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611516}}, and [[:en:Kyahan|Kyahan]], carrying [[:en:Drum|taiko]] and sticks. * '''Sasara-zuri''' (ささら摺り): Two boys aged 14–15 wearing [[:en:Kasuri|kasuri]] garments, equipped with [[:en:Binzasara|Binzasara]] (wooden clappers). * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several adult male performers in {{ill|Edo-tsuma|ja|江戸褄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo-tsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} kimono, wearing {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okoso zukin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} hoods and holding {{ill|yotsutake|ja|四つ竹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yotsutake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or sensu.
The performance begins with a speech by the Kuwakashira, followed by dance sequences symbolizing "Seed Sowing," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Milling," "Winnowing," and "Ending Ceremony." The ritual concludes with the Kuwakashira delivering congratulatory remarks.
== References == * "Matsuri, Geinō, Gyōji Daijiten (Vol. 2)" (朝倉書店, 2009), ISBN 978-4-254-50013-4.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:津島の田植踊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11556060]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a traditional folk performance of rice planting dance from {{ill|Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja|福島県双葉郡浪江町津島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Japan. It is recognized as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a prefectural {{ill|Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} of Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice planting dance is a form of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku performing arts|ja|予祝芸能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshuku performing arts|qq=}} that prays for abundant harvests, passed down through four villages in Namie: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akugun. It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 14th (lunar calendar)|1=14th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839082}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 15th (lunar calendar)|1=15th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838395}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 17th (lunar calendar)|1=17th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838983}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shrine]], followed by performances at houses upon request.
The performance features distinct roles: * '''Kuwagashira''' (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagajuban|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 襦袢|1=Zhongyi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55358825}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a [[:en:Tenugui|Tenugui]] around their head, and carries a large calabash. * '''Taneoroshi''' (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mon-tsuki haori hakama|1=montsuki-haori-hakama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605315}}, equi11ed with short swords and folding fans, who sing. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaku-himo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matabiki|1=momohiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611516}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. * '''Sasara-suri''' (ささら摺り): Two performers in [[:en:Kasuri|Kasuri]] kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo cla11ers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okoso zukin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion tier 3]] {{Japan-culture-stub}}
}}

[[Category:pages with merged translations]] ==Merged second translation== '''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice-planting dance|qq=}}, a form of folk performing art, passed down in {{ill|Tsunami, Namie|ja|浪江町津島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunami, Namie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Namie, Fukushima|Namie, Fukushima]], located in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]], Japan. It has been designated as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a {{ill|Prefectural Cultural Property of Japan|ja|都道府県指定無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Cultural Property of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice-planting dance is a {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝芸能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|qq=}} (ritual performance for blessings of bountiful harvests) originating from four [[:en:Human settlement|settlements]] in Tsushima: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akaogi. It was traditionally performed on the {{ill|14th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/14th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}, {{ill|15th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/15th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}, and {{ill|17th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/17th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}. Today, the dance is performed according to the Gregorian calendar. On the 14th, it is first offered at each settlement's [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]], followed by performances at requested households.
The performers include: * '''Kuwakashira''' (鍬頭): Dressed in {{ill|naga-juban|ja|長襦袢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/naga-juban|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 襦袢|1=Zhongyi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55358825}} and tied with {{ill|donko|ja|緞子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/donko|1=donko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11607976}}, wearing a twisted [[:en:Tenugui|tenugui]] headband and carrying a large gourd. * '''Tane-oroshi''' (種下ろし): Two singers dressed in {{ill|formal kimono|ja|紋付羽織袴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/formal kimono|1=montsuki-haori-hakama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605315}}, armed with a [[:en:Wakizashi|wakizashi]] and holding [[:en:Hand fan|sensu]]. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two drummers wearing {{ill|formal hakama|ja|袷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/formal hakama|1=awase|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626642}} tied with a {{ill|yaku-obi|ja|奴帯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yaku-obi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with rolled-up kimono hems, white {{ill|mohimo|ja|股引|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mohimo|1=momohiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611516}}, and [[:en:Kyahan|Kyahan]], carrying [[:en:Drum|taiko]] and sticks. * '''Sasara-zuri''' (ささら摺り): Two boys aged 14–15 wearing [[:en:Kasuri|kasuri]] garments, equipped with [[:en:Binzasara|Binzasara]] (wooden clappers). * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several adult male performers in {{ill|Edo-tsuma|ja|江戸褄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo-tsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} kimono, wearing {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okoso zukin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} hoods and holding {{ill|yotsutake|ja|四つ竹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yotsutake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or sensu.
The performance begins with a speech by the Kuwakashira, followed by dance sequences symbolizing "Seed Sowing," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Milling," "Winnowing," and "Ending Ceremony." The ritual concludes with the Kuwakashira delivering congratulatory remarks.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:津島の田植踊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11556060]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a traditional folk performance of rice planting dance from {{ill|Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja|福島県双葉郡浪江町津島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunami, Namie, Fukushima|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Japan. It is recognized as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a prefectural {{ill|Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} of Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice planting dance is a form of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku performing arts|ja|予祝芸能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshuku performing arts|qq=}} that prays for abundant harvests, passed down through four villages in Namie: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akugun. It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 14th (lunar calendar)|1=14th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839082}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 15th (lunar calendar)|1=15th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838395}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 17th (lunar calendar)|1=17th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838983}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shrine]], followed by performances at houses upon request.
The performance features distinct roles: * '''Kuwagashira''' (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagajuban|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 襦袢|1=Zhongyi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55358825}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a [[:en:Tenugui|Tenugui]] around their head, and carries a large calabash. * '''Taneoroshi''' (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mon-tsuki haori hakama|1=montsuki-haori-hakama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605315}}, equi11ed with short swords and folding fans, who sing. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaku-himo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matabiki|1=momohiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611516}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. * '''Sasara-suri''' (ささら摺り): Two performers in [[:en:Kasuri|Kasuri]] kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo cla11ers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okoso zukin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion tier 3]] {{Japan-culture-stub}}
}}

[[Category:pages with merged translations]] ==Merged second translation== '''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice-planting dance|qq=}}, a form of folk performing art, passed down in {{ill|Tsunami, Namie|ja|浪江町津島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunami, Namie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Namie, Fukushima|Namie, Fukushima]], located in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]], Japan. It has been designated as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a {{ill|Prefectural Cultural Property of Japan|ja|都道府県指定無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Cultural Property of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice-planting dance is a {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝芸能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|qq=}} (ritual performance for blessings of bountiful harvests) originating from four [[:en:Human settlement|settlements]] in Tsushima: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akaogi. It was traditionally performed on the {{ill|14th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/14th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}, {{ill|15th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/15th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}, and {{ill|17th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/17th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}. Today, the dance is performed according to the Gregorian calendar. On the 14th, it is first offered at each settlement's [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]], followed by performances at requested households.
The performers include: * '''Kuwakashira''' (鍬頭): Dressed in {{ill|naga-juban|ja|長襦袢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/naga-juban|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 襦袢|1=Zhongyi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55358825}} and tied with {{ill|donko|ja|緞子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/donko|1=donko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11607976}}, wearing a twisted [[:en:Tenugui|tenugui]] headband and carrying a large gourd. * '''Tane-oroshi''' (種下ろし): Two singers dressed in {{ill|formal kimono|ja|紋付羽織袴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/formal kimono|1=montsuki-haori-hakama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605315}}, armed with a [[:en:Wakizashi|wakizashi]] and holding [[:en:Hand fan|sensu]]. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two drummers wearing {{ill|formal hakama|ja|袷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/formal hakama|1=awase|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626642}} tied with a {{ill|yaku-obi|ja|奴帯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yaku-obi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with rolled-up kimono hems, white {{ill|mohimo|ja|股引|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mohimo|1=momohiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611516}}, and [[:en:Kyahan|Kyahan]], carrying [[:en:Drum|taiko]] and sticks. * '''Sasara-zuri''' (ささら摺り): Two boys aged 14–15 wearing [[:en:Kasuri|kasuri]] garments, equipped with [[:en:Binzasara|Binzasara]] (wooden clappers). * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several adult male performers in {{ill|Edo-tsuma|ja|江戸褄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo-tsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} kimono, wearing {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okoso zukin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} hoods and holding {{ill|yotsutake|ja|四つ竹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yotsutake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or sensu.
The performance begins with a speech by the Kuwakashira, followed by dance sequences symbolizing "Seed Sowing," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Milling," "Winnowing," and "Ending Ceremony." The ritual concludes with the Kuwakashira delivering congratulatory remarks.
== References == * "Matsuri, Geinō, Gyōji Daijiten (Vol. 2)" (朝倉書店, 2009), ISBN 978-4-254-50013-4.

It is recognized as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a prefectural {{ill|Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} of Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice planting dance is a form of {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku performing arts|ja|予祝芸能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshuku performing arts|qq=}} that prays for abundant harvests, passed down through four villages in Namie: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akugun. It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 14th (lunar calendar)|1=14th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839082}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 15th (lunar calendar)|1=15th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838395}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 17th (lunar calendar)|1=17th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838983}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shrine]], followed by performances at houses upon request.
The performance features distinct roles: * '''Kuwagashira''' (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagajuban|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 襦袢|1=Zhongyi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55358825}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a [[:en:Tenugui|Tenugui]] around their head, and carries a large calabash. * '''Taneoroshi''' (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mon-tsuki haori hakama|1=montsuki-haori-hakama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605315}}, equi11ed with short swords and folding fans, who sing. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaku-himo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matabiki|1=momohiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611516}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. * '''Sasara-suri''' (ささら摺り): Two performers in [[:en:Kasuri|Kasuri]] kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo cla11ers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okoso zukin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion tier 3]] {{Japan-culture-stub}}
}}

[[Category:pages with merged translations]] ==Merged second translation== '''Tsushima no Taue Odori''' (津島の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice-planting dance|qq=}}, a form of folk performing art, passed down in {{ill|Tsunami, Namie|ja|浪江町津島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunami, Namie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Namie, Fukushima|Namie, Fukushima]], located in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]], Japan. It has been designated as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}} by the national government and as a {{ill|Prefectural Cultural Property of Japan|ja|都道府県指定無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Cultural Property of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by Fukushima Prefecture.
This rice-planting dance is a {{ill|Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku|lt=Yoshuku-geino|ja|予祝芸能|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yoshuku-geino|qq=}} (ritual performance for blessings of bountiful harvests) originating from four [[:en:Human settlement|settlements]] in Tsushima: Kamitsushima, Shimotsushima, Minamitsushima, and Akaogi. It was traditionally performed on the {{ill|14th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/14th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}, {{ill|15th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/15th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}, and {{ill|17th day of the 1st lunar month|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/17th day of the 1st lunar month|qq=}}. Today, the dance is performed according to the Gregorian calendar. On the 14th, it is first offered at each settlement's [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]], followed by performances at requested households.
The performers include: * '''Kuwakashira''' (鍬頭): Dressed in {{ill|naga-juban|ja|長襦袢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/naga-juban|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 襦袢|1=Zhongyi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55358825}} and tied with {{ill|donko|ja|緞子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/donko|1=donko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11607976}}, wearing a twisted [[:en:Tenugui|tenugui]] headband and carrying a large gourd. * '''Tane-oroshi''' (種下ろし): Two singers dressed in {{ill|formal kimono|ja|紋付羽織袴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/formal kimono|1=montsuki-haori-hakama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605315}}, armed with a [[:en:Wakizashi|wakizashi]] and holding [[:en:Hand fan|sensu]]. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two drummers wearing {{ill|formal hakama|ja|袷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/formal hakama|1=awase|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626642}} tied with a {{ill|yaku-obi|ja|奴帯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yaku-obi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with rolled-up kimono hems, white {{ill|mohimo|ja|股引|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mohimo|1=momohiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611516}}, and [[:en:Kyahan|Kyahan]], carrying [[:en:Drum|taiko]] and sticks. * '''Sasara-zuri''' (ささら摺り): Two boys aged 14–15 wearing [[:en:Kasuri|kasuri]] garments, equipped with [[:en:Binzasara|Binzasara]] (wooden clappers). * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several adult male performers in {{ill|Edo-tsuma|ja|江戸褄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo-tsuma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} kimono, wearing {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okoso zukin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} hoods and holding {{ill|yotsutake|ja|四つ竹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yotsutake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or sensu.
The performance begins with a speech by the Kuwakashira, followed by dance sequences symbolizing "Seed Sowing," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Milling," "Winnowing," and "Ending Ceremony." The ritual concludes with the Kuwakashira delivering congratulatory remarks.
== References == * "Matsuri, Geinō, Gyōji Daijiten (Vol. 2)" (朝倉書店, 2009), ISBN 978-4-254-50013-4.

It was traditionally performed on {{ill|January 14th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月14日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 14th (lunar calendar)|1=14th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839082}}, {{ill|January 15th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 15th (lunar calendar)|1=15th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838395}}, and {{ill|January 17th (lunar calendar)|ja|1月17日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 17th (lunar calendar)|1=17th day of the 1st month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838983}} but is now conducted according to the Gregorian calendar. On the first day, January 14th, the dance is dedicated at each village's respective [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shrine]], followed by performances at houses upon request.
The performance features distinct roles: * '''Kuwagashira''' (鍬頭): The leader who wears a {{ill|Nagajuban|ja|長襦袢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagajuban|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 襦袢|1=Zhongyi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55358825}}, secures a brocade sash (緞子), twists a [[:en:Tenugui|Tenugui]] around their head, and carries a large calabash. * '''Taneoroshi''' (種下ろし): Two performers in {{ill|Mon-tsuki haori hakama|ja|紋付羽織袴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mon-tsuki haori hakama|1=montsuki-haori-hakama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605315}}, equi11ed with short swords and folding fans, who sing. * '''Taiko-uchi''' (太鼓打ち): Two performers in mon-tsuki, tied with a {{ill|Yaku-himo|ja|奴帯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaku-himo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who wear white {{ill|Matabiki|ja|股引|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matabiki|1=momohiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611516}} and gaiters, playing drums with sticks. * '''Sasara-suri''' (ささら摺り): Two performers in [[:en:Kasuri|Kasuri]] kimono, wearing short pants, and holding bamboo cla11ers (sasara). These roles are played by boys aged 14–15, while all other roles are performed by adult men. * '''Saotome''' (早乙女): Several performers wearing Edo-style tsumugi kimono and {{ill|Okoso zukin|ja|おこそ頭巾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okoso zukin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} headdresses, carrying small fans and four-legged bamboo sticks.
The dance begins with the Kuwagashira's prologue and progresses through themes such as "Seed Planting," "Rice Planting," "Harvesting," "Threshing," "Hulling," "Using the Winnowing Machine," and "Ritual Completion tier 3]] {{Japan-culture-stub}}
}}
